content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5601              322-6441              322-6444

April 20, 1979

Eric Hasseltine
Supervisor, District Five
Contra Costa County
120 S. Hartz Avenue
Denville, CA 94526

Re:  Opinion Request
     No. 79-004

Dear Mr. Hasseltine:

     Thank you for your letter of March 26, 1979, requesting
an opinion with respect to your obligations under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").
Since your letter does not raise a significant question of
interpretation under the Act, the Commission has decided to
issue a formal opinion on this matter. However, I hope the
following advice rendered pursuant to Government Code Section
83114(b) is helpful.

     Specifically, you asked whether you were prohibited
from accepting employment with a securities firm, a company
providing coffee service, a company designing and installing
energy conservation equipment for industry and a savings and
loan company. The Act does not regulate participation in
business endeavors. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit
you from accepting these employment opportunities. The Act,
however, is concerned with the making of governmental deci-
sions which foreseeably will have a material financial effect
on one's private financial interests. Accordingly, pursuant
to Government Code Sections 87100-87313 and the Conflict of
Interest Code for the Contra Costa Board of Supervisors, you
are required to file Statements of Economic Interests and
disqualify yourself from making, participating in, making
or using your official position to influence governmental
decisions in which you have a financial interest.

     The legal opinion which you received from your private
attorney properly describes the major conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act with one exception.

Eric Hasseltine
April 20, 1979
Page Two


It is true that the only remedy for a violation of the dis-
qualification provision, Government Code Section 87100, is
injunctive relief. However, a similar disqualification
provision exists in the Conflict of Interest Code approved
by the Commission for the Contra Costa Board of Supervisors.
Violation of this Code provision may subject you to civil,
criminal and administrative remedies.

You may also wish to consult with your attorney or the
Contra Costa County Counsel regarding other conflict of
interest laws such as Government Code Sections 1090, 1126
and the common law.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion re-
quest, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18321.

If you have any questions, please contact either Ted
Prim, Chief of our Conflicts of Interest Division, or me.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:kp

sections.question: Whether you were prohibited from accepting employment with a securities firm, a company providing coffee service, a company designing and installing energy conservation equipment for industry and a savings and loan company.
sections.conclusion: The Act does not regulate participation in business endeavors. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit you from accepting these employment opportunities. The Act, however, is concerned with the making of governmental decisions which foreseeably will have a material financial effect on one's private financial interests. Accordingly, pursuant to Government Code Sections 87100-87313 and the Conflict of Interest Code for the Contra Costa Board of Supervisors, you are required to file Statements of Economic Interests and disqualify yourself from making, participating in the making or using your official position to influence governmental decisions in which you have a financial interest.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Contra Costa County Supervisor can accept employment with a securities firm, coffee service company, energy conservation equipment company, and savings and loan company under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit accepting these employment opportunities, but the requestor must file Statements of Economic Interests and disqualify himself from governmental decisions in which he has a financial interest. The Conflict of Interest Code for the Contra Costa Board of Supervisors contains disqualification provisions whose violation may subject him to civil, criminal, and administrative remedies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is FPPC advice letter (Opinion Request No. 79-004) with clear question and conclusion sections. Marked as declined formal opinion but includes substantive advice per Government Code Section 83114(b).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether you were prohibited from accepting employment with a securities firm, a company providing coffee service, a company designing and installing energy conservation equipment for industry and a savings and loan company.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not regulate participation in business endeavors. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit you from accepting these employment opportunities. The Act, however, is concerned with the making of governmental decisions which foreseeably will have a material financial effect on one's private financial interests. Accordingly, pursuant to Government Code Sections 87100-87313 and the Conflict of Interest Code for the Contra Costa Board of Supervisors, you are required to file Statements of Economic Interests and disqualify yourself from making, participating in the making or using your official position to influence governmental decisions in which you have a financial interest.

embedding.summary: FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion but provided advice regarding a Contra Costa County Supervisor's obligations under the Political Reform Act regarding employment with multiple companies. The FPPC advised that while the Act does not prohibit the employment itself, the requestor must comply with conflict of interest rules including disqualification requirements.
